Two protein types: (1) Channel proteins — form pores/tunnels. Ion channels: voltage-gated, ligand-gated, mechanically-gated. Aquaporins: water channels (AQP1-13). Fast (~10⁷-10⁹ ions/sec for ion channels). (2) Carrier proteins — bind solute, conformational change, release on other side. Slower (~10²-10⁴ molecules/sec). GLUT transporters (GLUT1-5 for glucose). Amino acid transporters. Key properties: specificity (each protein transports specific molecules), saturation (rate plateaus at Vmax when all proteins occupied — similar to Michaelis-Menten enzyme kinetics), regulation (can be opened/closed, inserted/removed from membrane). Example: GLUT4 insertion in response to insulin signaling.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes tonicity?

options.text: The total concentration of all solutes in a solution, regardless of whether they can cross the membrane

The ability of a surrounding solution to cause a cell to gain or lose water — determined by the concentration of non-penetrating solutes (solutes that cannot cross the membrane) — a hypertonic solution causes water loss and cell shrinkage, a hypotonic solution causes water gain and cell swelling, and an isotonic solution results in no net water movement
The speed at which molecules diffuse across a membrane, measured in molecules per second
The electrical charge difference across the plasma membrane, also known as the membrane potential

quiz_questions.explanation: Tonicity: a measure of a solution's ability to cause a cell to gain or lose water. Depends ONLY on non-penetrating solutes (molecules that cannot cross the membrane — e.g., NaCl, sucrose). Distinguished from osmolarity (total solute concentration including penetrating solutes). Hypertonic: cell shrinks (more non-penetrating solute outside). Animal cells → crenation. Plant cells → plasmolysis. Hypotonic: cell swells (less non-penetrating solute outside). Animal cells → may lyse. Plant cells → turgid (cell wall prevents bursting). Isotonic: no net water movement. Example: 0.9% NaCl, 5% dextrose (penetrating) = isotonic to blood. Clinical: IV fluids must be isotonic to prevent hemolysis or crenation. Key subtlety: a solution can be hyperosmotic but isotonic if the excess solute penetrates the membrane (e.g., urea).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes plasmolysis?

options.text: The bursting of a plant cell when placed in a hypotonic solution due to excessive water intake

The shrinkage of the cell membrane away from the cell wall in a plant cell placed in a hypertonic solution — as water leaves the cell by osmosis, the cytoplasm contracts and the plasma membrane detaches from the rigid cell wall — plasmolysis causes wilting in plants and can lead to cell death if prolonged — it is reversible in early stages if the cell is returned to a hypotonic environment
The process of a cell dividing into two daughter cells during mitosis
The active pumping of water out of a cell using ATP energy, found exclusively in animal cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Plasmolysis: contraction of the protoplast (cell contents + membrane) away from the cell wall in a plant cell placed in a hypertonic environment. Mechanism: hypertonic solution (more solute outside) → water leaves cell by osmosis → cell volume decreases → plasma membrane detaches from cell wall (cell wall is rigid, does not shrink with the membrane) → space between membrane and wall fills with the hypertonic solution. Stages: incipient plasmolysis (membrane just beginning to detach) → evident plasmolysis → convex plasmolysis (severe, protoplast rounded). Reversible in early stages (deplasmolysis) if cells returned to hypotonic solution. Biological significance: explains wilting of plants in drought or salty soil. Lab demonstration: Elodea leaf in concentrated salt or sucrose solution — observe under microscope. Opposite: turgor/turgidity — water enters hypotonic solution → membrane pushes against cell wall → turgor pressure → cell rigid and upright.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes turgor pressure?

options.text: The pressure exerted by the cell membrane on the extracellular fluid in an animal cell, causing it to swell and burst

The pressure exerted by the cell contents (water-filled vacuole and cytoplasm) pushing outward against the rigid cell wall in plant cells — generated when water enters the cell by osmosis in a hypotonic environment — turgor pressure is essential for maintaining plant structural rigidity, driving cell elongation during growth, and controlling stomatal opening and closing via guard cells
The force generated by ATP hydrolysis during active transport of sodium and potassium ions
The pressure created when gases diffuse across the alveolar membrane in the lungs during respiration

quiz_questions.explanation: Turgor pressure (pressure potential, Ψp): the pressure of the cell contents pushing outward against the cell wall. Generated by osmotic water uptake: hypotonic environment → water enters by osmosis → cell swells → membrane and contents push against rigid cell wall → wall pushes back (wall pressure) → equilibrium. Functions: structural support (non-woody plants rely on turgor for upright posture — wilting = loss of turgor), cell growth (acid growth hypothesis: auxin activates H⁺ ATPase → cell wall loosens → turgor drives expansion), stomatal regulation (guard cells: K⁺ pumped in → water follows by osmosis → guard cells swell and curve apart → stoma opens; K⁺ leaves → water follows → guard cells deflate → stoma closes). Water potential equation: Ψ = Ψs + Ψp. At full turgor: Ψp is positive and large, offsetting negative Ψs → Ψ approaches 0 → no further net water entry.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hospital patient accidentally receives an IV drip of distilled water instead of isotonic saline (0.9% NaCl). The patient rapidly develops hemolysis and hemoglobinuria (hemoglobin in the urine). Using your knowledge of osmosis and tonicity, explain the sequence of events from IV infusion to kidney damage.

options.text: Distilled water is hypertonic — it causes red blood cells to shrink and crenate, leading to clotting in the kidneys

Distilled water is extremely hypotonic (0 mOsm) relative to blood (~300 mOsm) — water enters red blood cells rapidly by osmosis through aquaporin-1 channels → RBCs swell beyond membrane capacity → hemolysis (membrane ruptures) → free hemoglobin released into plasma → hemoglobin is filtered by kidney glomeruli but is toxic to renal tubular epithelial cells → acute tubular necrosis → potential acute kidney injury — additionally, potassium released from lysed cells → hyperkalemia → risk of cardiac arrhythmia
Distilled water kills bacteria in the blood, which then release toxins that attack the kidneys — this has nothing to do with osmosis
Distilled water is isotonic to blood — the hemolysis must be caused by a contamination in the IV tubing rather than a tonicity effect

quiz_questions.explanation: Hypotonic hemolysis — clinical scenario: (1) Distilled water (0 mOsm) vs. blood (~300 mOsm) → extremely hypotonic. (2) Water enters RBCs via AQP1 aquaporins and slowly through the lipid bilayer → osmotic swelling. RBCs have no cell wall, cannot resist over-expansion. (3) Hemolysis: membrane ruptures → hemoglobin (Hb) released into plasma. Free Hb: saturates haptoglobin binding capacity → excess free Hb filtered by kidneys → Hb precipitates in renal tubules (especially under acidic conditions) → mechanical obstruction + direct toxicity to tubular epithelial cells → acute tubular necrosis → acute kidney injury (AKI). (4) Hyperkalemia: lysed RBCs release intracellular K⁺ ([K⁺]ᵢ ≈ 140 mM) → plasma [K⁺] rises → disrupts cardiac membrane potential → risk of fatal arrhythmia. (5) Prevention: all IV fluids must be isotonic (0.9% NaCl = 308 mOsm, Ringer's lactate = 273 mOsm) or carefully formulated to avoid osmotic cell damage. (6) Plant cell comparison: would not burst due to cell wall providing turgor pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places three groups of de-shelled eggs (using vinegar to dissolve the shell, leaving only the semipermeable membrane) into: (A) distilled water, (B) corn syrup, and (C) isotonic saline. After 24 hours, she measures mass changes. Predict and explain the results using osmosis principles.

options.text: All three eggs will remain unchanged because de-shelled eggs cannot undergo osmosis — the semipermeable membrane blocks all water movement

Egg A (distilled water): gains mass and swells — water enters by osmosis because distilled water is hypotonic relative to egg contents. Egg B (corn syrup): loses mass and shrinks — water exits by osmosis because corn syrup is hypertonic (high sugar concentration). Egg C (isotonic saline): mass stays approximately the same — no net water movement because solute concentration is roughly equal inside and outside
Egg A shrinks, Egg B swells, and Egg C dissolves — distilled water pulls water out of cells by reverse osmosis while corn syrup pushes water in
All three eggs dissolve because removing the shell eliminates all protection — the semipermeable membrane cannot maintain osmotic balance

quiz_questions.explanation: De-shelled egg osmosis experiment: (1) Preparation: vinegar (acetic acid) dissolves CaCO₃ shell → exposes the semipermeable vitelline/inner membrane. (2) Egg A (distilled water — hypotonic): water enters egg by osmosis (high water potential outside → low water potential inside) → egg swells, mass increases (~20-30%). Membrane may feel taut. (3) Egg B (corn syrup — hypertonic, ~70% sugar): water exits egg by osmosis (low water potential in corn syrup due to high solute) → egg shrinks, mass decreases (~20-30%). Membrane wrinkled, egg feels soft and smaller. (4) Egg C (isotonic saline — ~equal osmolarity): no net water movement → mass ~unchanged. (5) This is a classic AP Biology lab demonstrating: osmosis, tonicity, selectively permeable membranes, and the relationship between solute concentration and water movement. Quantitative analysis: percent change in mass = (final mass − initial mass)/initial mass × 100.

quiz_questions.question_text: SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g., empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) are a class of diabetes drugs that block the sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 in the kidney proximal tubule. Explain how blocking this secondary active transporter helps lower blood glucose levels.

options.text: SGLT2 inhibitors increase insulin production from pancreatic β cells — they have nothing to do with glucose transport in the kidney

Normally, SGLT2 (a Na⁺-glucose symporter in the kidney proximal tubule) reabsorbs ~90% of filtered glucose back into the blood using the Na⁺ gradient established by the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase (secondary active transport) — SGLT2 inhibitors block this cotransporter → glucose remains in the tubular fluid → excreted in urine (glycosuria) → blood glucose levels decrease — this mechanism is independent of insulin, making these drugs effective even in advanced Type 2 diabetes with severe insulin resistance
SGLT2 inhibitors work by increasing glucose diffusion across all cell membranes — they eliminate the need for both insulin and GLUT transporters entirely
These drugs prevent glucose absorption in the intestines by blocking GLUT2 transporters — kidney function is not involved

quiz_questions.explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors and secondary active transport: (1) Normal kidney glucose handling: ~180 g glucose filtered by glomeruli daily → nearly all reabsorbed in proximal tubule. SGLT2 (in early proximal tubule): low affinity, high capacity, reabsorbs ~90% (1 Na⁺: 1 glucose symport). SGLT1 (in late proximal tubule): high affinity, low capacity, reabsorbs remaining ~10% (2 Na⁺: 1 glucose). Both powered by Na⁺ gradient from basolateral Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase. Glucose exits tubular cells via GLUT2 (basolateral). (2) SGLT2 inhibitors (gliflozins): block SGLT2 → ~70-80 g glucose/day excreted in urine → lowers blood glucose by ~30-50 mg/dL. (3) Additional benefits: weight loss (caloric loss via glucosuria), blood pressure reduction (osmotic diuresis, natriuresis), cardiovascular protection (reduced heart failure hospitalization — landmark EMPA-REG OUTCOME trial for empagliflozin), kidney protection. (4) Side effects: urinary tract infections, genital yeast infections (glucose-rich urine = bacterial/fungal growth medium), diabetic ketoacidosis (rare). (5) This is a direct clinical application of secondary active transport principles.

quiz_questions.question_text: A farmer waters his crops with irrigation water that has become increasingly salty due to mineral buildup. Over several weeks, the crops wilt and die despite receiving adequate water volume. Using your knowledge of osmosis, plasmolysis, and water potential, explain why salty irrigation water kills the plants.

options.text: Salt is toxic to chloroplasts and directly destroys the photosynthetic apparatus — osmosis and water potential play no role in salt damage

Salty irrigation water is hypertonic to plant root cells → water potential of soil solution becomes more negative than root cell water potential → water moves OUT of root cells by osmosis instead of in → root cells lose turgor pressure → plasmolysis → plant cannot absorb water → leaves wilt (loss of turgor in leaf cells) → prolonged water stress → stomata close (guard cells lose turgor) → photosynthesis stops → plant dies — this is called 'physiological drought' because water is physically present but osmotically unavailable
Salt makes the water too heavy for roots to absorb — this is a physical weight problem, not an osmotic issue
The salt fills in spaces between soil particles, physically blocking water from reaching the roots — osmosis plays no role in this process

quiz_questions.explanation: Salt stress and osmotic water relations in plants: (1) Normal water uptake: soil water potential (Ψ) > root cell Ψ → water enters roots by osmosis → moves through plant via transpiration stream. (2) Salty soil: dissolved salts (NaCl, etc.) lower soil water potential (more negative Ψs) → if soil Ψ < root Ψ → water flows OUT of roots → cells lose turgor → plasmolysis. (3) 'Physiological drought': plants experience water stress even though soil is wet — water is osmotically unavailable. (4) Cascade of effects: root water loss → reduced turgor throughout plant → wilting → stomatal closure (guard cells lose turgor) → reduced CO₂ uptake → reduced photosynthesis → reduced growth → death. (5) Ion toxicity: Na⁺ and Cl⁻ accumulate in cells → disrupt enzyme function, protein structure, membrane integrity. (6) Soil salinization: major agricultural problem worldwide (affects ~20% of irrigated land). (7) Plant adaptations: halophytes (salt-tolerant plants) accumulate compatible solutes (proline, glycine betaine) to lower cellular Ψs without toxic effects → maintain water uptake even in salty soil. Mangroves: salt-secreting glands.

quiz_questions.question_text: A neuroscientist explains that the resting membrane potential of a neuron (~-70 mV) depends on the ion gradients established by the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase and on K⁺ leak channels. How does the interplay between active transport and passive transport create this electrical potential?

options.text: The resting membrane potential is created solely by passive diffusion of all ions through the membrane — no active transport is involved

The Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase (active transport) establishes steep ion gradients: high K⁺ inside (~140 mM), low outside (~5 mM); high Na⁺ outside (~145 mM), low inside (~12 mM) — K⁺ leak channels allow K⁺ to passively diffuse out of the cell down its concentration gradient → positive charge leaves → inside becomes negative relative to outside → this creates the resting potential (~-70 mV) — the pump also directly contributes by being electrogenic (3 Na⁺ out vs. 2 K⁺ in = net +1 charge exported per cycle), accounting for about -5 to -10 mV of the resting potential
The resting potential is created exclusively by the Na⁺/K⁺ pump exporting positive charges — K⁺ leak channels play no role whatsoever
Membrane potential only exists during an action potential — neurons have zero electrical potential when at rest

quiz_questions.explanation: Resting membrane potential — active + passive transport interplay: (1) Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase (active) creates and maintains gradients: [K⁺]ᵢ ≈ 140 mM, [K⁺]ₒ ≈ 5 mM; [Na⁺]ₒ ≈ 145 mM, [Na⁺]ᵢ ≈ 12 mM. (2) Resting membrane has many open K⁺ leak channels but few Na⁺ channels → membrane is ~50-75× more permeable to K⁺ than Na⁺ at rest. (3) K⁺ diffuses OUT through leak channels (down concentration gradient) → positive charge leaves cell → interior becomes negative. This is facilitated diffusion (passive). (4) K⁺ efflux continues until the electrical gradient (inside negative, pulling K⁺ back in) balances the concentration gradient (pushing K⁺ out) → electrochemical equilibrium for K⁺ → Nernst potential for K⁺ ≈ -90 mV. (5) Small Na⁺ leak inward moves potential slightly more positive → actual resting potential ≈ -70 mV (Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz equation). (6) Electrogenic contribution of pump: 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in → net +1 charge exported per cycle → contributes ~-5 to -10 mV directly. (7) Key concept: active transport creates the gradients; passive transport (K⁺ leak) creates the voltage.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cystic fibrosis patients have defective CFTR chloride channels, leading to thick mucus in the lungs. A research team is studying how blocking a chloride channel can lead to altered water balance. Explain the transport principles linking CFTR dysfunction to mucus dehydration.

options.text: CFTR produces mucus directly — when it is defective, the mucus it produces is thicker because the protein structure is wrong

Normal CFTR channels secrete Cl⁻ ions into the airway lumen (active transport, ATP-gated channel) and simultaneously inhibit epithelial Na⁺ channels (ENaC) — when CFTR is defective (most commonly ΔF508 mutation → misfolded protein degraded before reaching membrane): Cl⁻ secretion stops AND Na⁺ absorption increases uncontrollably (ENaC no longer inhibited) → Na⁺ moves from lumen into cells → water follows Na⁺ by osmosis → airway surface liquid becomes dehydrated → mucus layer thickens → impaired mucociliary clearance → chronic bacterial infections (Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Staphylococcus aureus)
CFTR deficiency causes the phospholipid bilayer to become impermeable to all molecules — cells cannot exchange any substance with the airway lumen
Thick mucus is caused by excess cholesterol in lung cell membranes — CFTR and chloride transport are unrelated to mucus hydration

quiz_questions.explanation: CFTR and osmotic water balance in airways: (1) Normal: CFTR (Cl⁻ channel + ENaC regulator) on apical membrane of airway epithelial cells. Cl⁻ secreted into lumen → Na⁺ follows paracellularly (electrical attraction) → water follows by osmosis → maintains thin, hydrated airway surface liquid (ASL, ~7 μm deep) → proper mucociliary clearance (~12-15 mm/min). (2) CF (ΔF508 = ~70% of mutations): CFTR protein misfolds → degraded by proteasome → never reaches plasma membrane → no Cl⁻ secretion + ENaC unregulated → excessive Na⁺ absorption from lumen → water follows Na⁺ out of lumen by osmosis → ASL dehydrated to ~3 μm → mucus becomes viscous and sticky → cilia cannot beat effectively → mucus stagnates → bacterial colonization → chronic inflammation → bronchiectasis → progressive lung destruction. (3) Treatment: CFTR modulators (Trikafta® = elexacaftor + tezacaftor + ivacaftor) — correct folding and enhance channel function of ΔF508-CFTR. (4) Key transport principles: channel protein function, ion transport driving osmotic water movement, coupling of different transport processes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Paramecium is a freshwater protist that lives in a hypotonic environment. It uses a contractile vacuole to continuously pump out water that enters by osmosis. Without this organelle, the cell would swell and burst. What transport principles explain why the contractile vacuole is essential, and why do marine protists lack one?

options.text: Paramecium actively pumps water INTO its cell and the contractile vacuole stores it — marine protists don't need one because salt water inhibits cell growth

Freshwater is hypotonic to Paramecium's cytoplasm → water enters continuously by osmosis (through the membrane and aquaporin-like channels) → without a mechanism to expel excess water, the cell would swell and lyse (no cell wall to provide counter-pressure) → the contractile vacuole collects excess water and actively pumps it out (requires ATP) at rates of 1-2 vacuole cycles per minute — marine protists live in an approximately isotonic environment (seawater ~1000 mOsm), so little net water enters by osmosis and no contractile vacuole is needed
The contractile vacuole is used for digestion, not water balance — marine protists have different digestive enzymes that replace it
Freshwater protists use active transport of salt ions to prevent osmosis entirely — the contractile vacuole is vestigial and nonfunctional

quiz_questions.explanation: Contractile vacuole and osmoregulation: (1) Problem: Paramecium cytoplasm (~80-100 mOsm) is hypertonic relative to freshwater (~0-5 mOsm) → water enters continuously by osmosis → without countermeasure, cell swells and lyses. (2) Solution: contractile vacuole — a specialized organelle that collects excess cytoplasmic water and actively expels it. Mechanism: collecting tubules (radiating canals) gather water → feed into central vacuole → vacuole fills (diastole) → contracts (systole) → water expelled through a pore. Requires ATP (V-type H⁺ ATPase + aquaporins). Rate: ~1-2 cycles/min; expels cell volume equivalent every 15-20 min. (3) Marine protists: seawater (~1000 mOsm) is approximately isotonic or hypertonic to cytoplasm → minimal net osmotic water entry → contractile vacuoles absent or vestigial. (4) Analogous to kidneys in animals: kidneys regulate body fluid osmolarity (countercurrent multiplier in loop of Henle, ADH-regulated aquaporin insertion in collecting duct). (5) Demonstrates: osmosis in hypotonic environments, cellular adaptations to osmotic stress, ATP-dependent water expulsion.

quiz_questions.question_text: During organ transplant surgery, donor organs are preserved in special solutions (e.g., University of Wisconsin solution, Custodiol) rather than in normal saline or plain water. These solutions are carefully formulated to match intracellular ionic composition and osmolarity. Using your knowledge of transport, explain why the preservation solution matters.

options.text: Preservation solutions are simply antibiotics that kill bacteria on the organ surface — the ionic composition has no effect on organ viability

Without functioning Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase (ATP depleted in ischemic organ), ion gradients collapse → Na⁺ leaks into cells, K⁺ leaks out → water follows Na⁺ into cells by osmosis → cellular edema (swelling) → membrane damage. Preservation solutions contain: high K⁺ (matches intracellular [K⁺] to reduce K⁺ efflux), impermeant anions like lactobionate and raffinose (maintain osmotic balance, prevent water influx), and low Na⁺ and Ca²⁺ — this slows gradient dissipation and prevents osmotic swelling during the hours between harvesting and transplant
Organs can be stored in any solution including tap water — the chemical composition of the preservation fluid has no effect on transplant success
Preservation solutions work by freezing the organ solid, and their ionic composition is irrelevant — it's the low temperature alone that prevents deterioration

quiz_questions.explanation: Organ preservation and transport principles: (1) Problem: harvested organ → ischemia (no blood flow) → ATP depletion → Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase stops → ion gradients collapse. Without the pump: Na⁺ (high outside) leaks in through Na⁺ channels → water follows by osmosis → cell swelling → membrane disruption → irreversible injury. Ca²⁺ (high outside, low inside cytoplasm) leaks in → activates destructive enzymes (phospholipases, proteases, endonucleases) → cell death. (2) UW solution (Belzer, 1987): high K⁺ (~125 mM, matches intracellular) → reduces K⁺ efflux through leak channels. Impermeant anions (lactobionate, raffinose, hydroxyethyl starch) → maintain extracellular osmolarity → prevent osmotic cell swelling. Low Na⁺ → reduces Na⁺ influx. Glutathione → antioxidant. Adenosine → ATP precursor for recovery. (3) Result: extends cold ischemia tolerance: kidney 24-36 hours, liver 12-18 hours, heart 4-6 hours. (4) Demonstrates: passive transport (ion leak when pumps fail), osmosis driving cell swelling, and how understanding transport principles directly saves lives in transplant medicine.

quiz_questions.question_text: A biology student observes that a wilted celery stalk placed in a glass of fresh water becomes crisp and rigid again within a few hours. However, a celery stalk placed in concentrated salt water becomes even more wilted. Explain both observations using the concepts of osmosis, turgor pressure, and plasmolysis.

options.text: The fresh water chemically reacts with cellulose in the cell wall to make it rigid — the salt water dissolves the cell wall, causing additional wilting

Fresh water (hypotonic): water enters celery cells by osmosis → vacuoles fill → turgor pressure increases → cells push against rigid cell walls → stalk becomes crisp and rigid (recovery from incipient plasmolysis/wilting). Salt water (hypertonic): water leaves cells by osmosis → vacuoles shrink → turgor pressure drops → plasma membrane pulls away from cell wall (plasmolysis) → cells lose structural support → stalk becomes even more wilted and limp
Celery absorbs all available salt through active transport, making its cells too heavy to remain rigid — this is a gravitational effect, not osmosis
Fresh water kills bacteria on the celery surface, allowing it to heal — salt water promotes bacterial growth that destroys the cell structure

quiz_questions.explanation: Celery and osmosis — everyday example: (1) Wilted celery: cells have lost water (dehydration, perhaps from storage) → reduced turgor pressure → cells are flaccid → stalk is limp. (2) In fresh water (hypotonic): pure water has higher water potential (Ψ) than celery cells (which contain dissolved salts, sugars, acids → negative Ψs). Water enters cells by osmosis → central vacuoles refill → volume increases → plasma membrane pushes against cell wall → turgor pressure restored → cell wall provides structural rigidity → stalk is crisp. (3) In salt water (hypertonic): salt solution has lower Ψ than celery cells → water exits cells by osmosis → vacuoles shrink → membrane pulls away from cell wall (plasmolysis) → turgor lost → cells flaccid → stalk even more wilted. (4) Everyday food science applications: salting vegetables (drawing out water — pickling, sauerkraut); marinating (osmotic exchange of flavors); crispy lettuce (store in cold water). (5) Demonstrates: osmosis, water potential, turgor pressure, plasmolysis/deplasmolysis — all fundamental cellular transport concepts with tangible real-world observations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Cholera toxin produced by Vibrio cholerae causes massive, life-threatening diarrhea by permanently activating CFTR chloride channels in intestinal epithelial cells. Oral rehydration therapy (ORT) — a simple solution of water, salt, and sugar — saves millions of lives by exploiting a specific membrane transport protein to rehydrate patients. What is the transport mechanism behind ORT?

options.text: ORT works simply by replacing lost water through passive absorption in the stomach — the salt and sugar are only for taste

Cholera toxin locks CFTR Cl⁻ channels open → massive Cl⁻ secretion into intestinal lumen → Na⁺ follows paracellularly → water follows by osmosis → severe watery diarrhea (up to 20 L/day). ORT exploits the Na⁺-glucose cotransporter SGLT1 (secondary active transport, unaffected by cholera toxin): glucose + Na⁺ enter intestinal cells together via SGLT1 → water follows by osmosis → rehydration occurs even while the secretory diarrhea continues — this is why the specific ratio of salt to sugar is critical and why plain water alone is insufficient
ORT works by killing Vibrio cholerae bacteria directly — the sugar provides energy to immune cells and the salt creates a hostile environment for the pathogen
ORT rehydrates patients through active transport of water molecules using ATP — the salt and sugar provide the energy needed to pump water back into cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Cholera and oral rehydration therapy — transport principles saving lives: (1) Cholera pathogenesis: V. cholerae colonizes small intestine → secretes cholera toxin → toxin enters epithelial cells → permanently activates Gsα → constitutive cAMP production → CFTR Cl⁻ channels locked open → massive Cl⁻ secretion into lumen → Na⁺ follows (electrochemical attraction) → water follows by osmosis → profuse rice-water diarrhea (up to 20 L/day) → severe dehydration → hypovolemic shock → death within hours without treatment. (2) ORT mechanism: the Na⁺-glucose cotransporter SGLT1 on the apical membrane of intestinal enterocytes is NOT affected by cholera toxin. SGLT1: secondary active transporter — 2 Na⁺ + 1 glucose enter cell together (symport, powered by Na⁺ gradient from basolateral Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase). When patient drinks ORT solution (specific ratio: ~3.5 g NaCl, 20 g glucose per liter water, WHO formula): glucose + Na⁺ absorbed via SGLT1 → increases intracellular osmolarity → water follows by osmosis → net fluid absorption even while secretory mechanism is still active. (3) Impact: ORT called 'potentially the most important medical advance of the 20th century' (The Lancet, 1978). Reduces cholera mortality from >50% to <1%. Saves ~1 million lives per year globally. (4) Key transport concepts demonstrated: secondary active transport (SGLT1), osmosis driven by solute absorption, coupling of Na⁺ and glucose transport, clinical application of basic membrane transport biology.

summary_sections.title: Key Concepts: Structures and Organelles
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Concepts: Structures and Organelles

This lesson covers the fundamental concepts of structures and organelles in biology.

  • Understanding the basic principles and terminology
  • Exploring real-world applications and examples
  • Connecting to other biological concepts

Learning Objectives

  • Define key terms related to structures and organelles
  • Explain the importance of this topic in biology
  • Apply concepts to solve problems

Why This Matters

  • Builds foundation for advanced biology topics
  • Has practical applications in medicine and research
  • Helps explain natural phenomena

flashcards.definition: Rough ER: protein synthesis/modification; Smooth ER: lipid synthesis/detox.
Modifies, sorts, and packages proteins and lipids for transport.
Organelle containing digestive enzymes to break down waste and foreign material.
Organelle in plant cells where photosynthesis occurs; contains chlorophyll.
Storage organelle; large central vacuole in plant cells maintains turgor pressure.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the two types of endoplasmic reticulum, and how do their structures relate to their functions?

options.text: Both rough and smooth ER are identical in structure and function — they both produce lipids and have ribosomes on their surface

Rough ER (RER) has ribosomes studding its outer surface and functions in synthesis, folding, and modification of membrane-bound and secretory proteins — Smooth ER (SER) lacks ribosomes and functions in lipid (phospholipid, steroid hormone) synthesis, calcium ion storage, and detoxification of drugs and poisons — George Palade (Nobel Prize 1974) used electron microscopy and cell fractionation to elucidate ER structure and the secretory pathway
Rough ER produces lipids because phospholipids are rough in texture, while smooth ER produces proteins because protein surfaces are smooth
The endoplasmic reticulum is found only in plant cells and has no equivalent in animal cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Endoplasmic reticulum (ER) — two types: (1) Rough ER (RER): studded with ribosomes on cytoplasmic face. Function: synthesizes proteins destined for membranes, secretion, or lysosomes. Signal sequence on nascent polypeptide → signal recognition particle (SRP) → docks at SRP receptor on RER → protein threaded into ER lumen via translocon → folding (chaperones like BiP, calnexin) → glycosylation (N-linked sugars added) → quality control → transport vesicles bud off → Golgi. (2) Smooth ER (SER): no ribosomes. Functions: phospholipid synthesis (all membranes receive lipids from SER), steroid hormone synthesis (abundant in adrenal cortex, testes, ovaries), detoxification of drugs/alcohol/poisons (cytochrome P450 enzymes — abundant in liver hepatocytes → liver SER proliferates with chronic alcohol/drug exposure), calcium storage (sarcoplasmic reticulum in muscle = specialized SER). George Palade (Nobel 1974): pioneered electron microscopy of organelles and discovered the secretory pathway.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Golgi apparatus, how is it organized, and what are its functions in the cell?

options.text: The Golgi apparatus is a single membrane sac that stores DNA and is responsible for cell division — it has no role in protein processing

The Golgi apparatus (discovered by Camillo Golgi, Nobel Prize 1906) is a stack of flattened membrane sacs (cisternae) organized with a cis face (receiving side, facing ER) and a trans face (shipping side, facing plasma membrane) — it modifies proteins and lipids (glycosylation, phosphorylation, proteolytic cleavage), sorts them by adding molecular 'address tags' (e.g., mannose-6-phosphate for lysosomal enzymes), and packages them into vesicles for transport to their destinations (plasma membrane, lysosomes, secretion)
The Golgi is identical to the endoplasmic reticulum — they are the same organelle with different names used in different countries
The Golgi apparatus is found only in prokaryotic cells and functions in peptidoglycan synthesis for the cell wall

quiz_questions.explanation: Golgi apparatus (Golgi complex/body): (1) Discovered by Camillo Golgi (1898) using silver staining; Nobel Prize in Physiology/Medicine 1906 (shared with Ramón y Cajal). (2) Structure: stack of 4-8 flattened membrane cisternae. Polarized: cis face (receiving — near ER, receives transport vesicles from ER) → medial cisternae → trans face (shipping — facing plasma membrane, trans-Golgi network or TGN). (3) Functions: modification (O-linked glycosylation, modification of N-linked sugars from ER, phosphorylation — mannose-6-phosphate tag for lysosomal enzymes, sulfation, proteolytic processing — e.g., proinsulin → insulin), sorting (molecular address tags direct proteins to correct destinations), packaging (trans-Golgi network buds vesicles targeted to: plasma membrane for secretion or membrane insertion, lysosomes, or regulated secretory granules). (4) Vesicle trafficking: COPI vesicles (retrograde, Golgi → ER), COPII vesicles (anterograde, ER → Golgi), clathrin-coated vesicles (TGN → lysosomes, endosomes). James Rothman, Randy Schekman, Thomas Südhof — Nobel Prize 2013 for vesicle trafficking.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are lysosomes, how are they formed, and what roles do they play in the cell?

options.text: Lysosomes are organelles that produce ATP by oxidizing glucose through aerobic respiration — they contain enzymes for the citric acid cycle

Lysosomes (discovered by Christian de Duve, Nobel Prize 1974) are membrane-bound organelles containing ~50 hydrolytic enzymes (proteases, lipases, nucleases, glycosidases) that operate at an acidic pH (~4.5-5.0, maintained by V-type H⁺ ATPase proton pumps) — they digest materials brought in by endocytosis (foreign material, pathogens) and by autophagy (damaged organelles, worn-out proteins) — lysosomal enzyme deficiencies cause ~50 lysosomal storage diseases (e.g., Tay-Sachs, Gaucher disease)
Lysosomes are found only in plant cells where they function to store water and maintain turgor pressure — animal cells use different organelles for digestion
Lysosomes are free-floating enzymes in the cytoplasm that have no membrane barrier — they digest cellular waste by direct contact

quiz_questions.explanation: Lysosomes — the cell's digestive system: (1) Discovery: Christian de Duve (Nobel Prize 1974) discovered lysosomes using cell fractionation and centrifugation. (2) Formation: lysosomal enzymes synthesized on RER → glycosylated → Golgi adds mannose-6-phosphate (M6P) tag → M6P receptors in TGN sort enzymes into clathrin-coated vesicles → delivered to late endosomes → mature into lysosomes. (3) Internal environment: pH 4.5-5.0 maintained by V-type H⁺ ATPase (proton pump — active transport). Acidic pH optimal for hydrolase activity; if lysosome ruptures, enzymes inactive at cytoplasmic pH 7.2 (safety mechanism). (4) Functions: (a) Heterophagy: digestion of extracellular material from endocytosis/phagocytosis. (b) Autophagy (Yoshinori Ohsumi, Nobel 2016): degradation/recycling of damaged organelles and aggregated proteins. Autophagosome engulfs target → fuses with lysosome → digestion → components recycled. Essential for cellular homeostasis. (5) Lysosomal storage diseases (~50 known): enzyme deficiency → substrate accumulates → cell dysfunction. Tay-Sachs (hexosaminidase A deficiency → GM2 ganglioside accumulation in neurons), Gaucher (glucocerebrosidase deficiency), Pompe (acid maltase deficiency).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the endomembrane system, and how do its components work together to produce, modify, and deliver proteins?

options.text: The endomembrane system is limited to the plasma membrane and has no internal components — proteins are made and delivered without any organelle cooperation

The endomembrane system is a network of interconnected membrane-bound organelles — including the nuclear envelope, rough ER, smooth ER, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, vesicles, and the plasma membrane — that work together in the secretory pathway: genes in the nucleus → mRNA → ribosomes on rough ER synthesize proteins → proteins folded and modified in ER lumen → COPII transport vesicles carry proteins to Golgi cis face → Golgi further modifies, sorts, and packages → trans-Golgi network sends vesicles to final destination (plasma membrane, lysosomes, or secretion)
The endomembrane system consists only of mitochondria and chloroplasts — these are the only organelles with internal membranes
The endomembrane system is a term used only in prokaryotic cells to describe their plasma membrane invaginations

quiz_questions.explanation: The endomembrane system: a connected network of organelles related by direct physical continuity or vesicle transfer. Components: (1) Nuclear envelope: double membrane, continuous with rough ER, nuclear pores regulate traffic. (2) Rough ER: protein synthesis and initial modification. (3) Smooth ER: lipid synthesis, detox, Ca²⁺ storage. (4) Golgi apparatus: further modification, sorting, packaging. (5) Lysosomes: intracellular digestion. (6) Vesicles: transport containers between compartments. (7) Plasma membrane: receives vesicles via exocytosis, sends material in via endocytosis. The secretory pathway: nucleus (gene → mRNA) → RER (translation, folding, glycosylation) → COPII vesicles → Golgi cis face → medial Golgi → trans-Golgi network (sorting) → clathrin or secretory vesicles → destination. NOT part of endomembrane system: mitochondria, chloroplasts (have own DNA, double membranes from endosymbiosis — not derived from ER/Golgi), peroxisomes (may derive from ER but functionally independent). Nobel Prizes: Palade (1974 — secretory pathway), Rothman/Schekman/Südhof (2013 — vesicle trafficking).

quiz_questions.question_text: What are chloroplasts, and what evidence supports the endosymbiotic theory of their origin?

options.text: Chloroplasts are found in all living cells and are responsible for cellular respiration — they evolved independently without any bacterial ancestor

Chloroplasts are double-membrane organelles in plant and algal cells where photosynthesis occurs (light reactions in thylakoid membranes, Calvin cycle in stroma) — the endosymbiotic theory (championed by Lynn Margulis, 1967) proposes that chloroplasts were once free-living photosynthetic cyanobacteria engulfed by an ancestral eukaryotic cell — evidence includes: double membranes, own circular DNA (similar to cyanobacterial genomes), own 70S ribosomes (same as bacteria), binary fission reproduction, and phylogenetic analysis showing chloroplast DNA is most closely related to cyanobacteria
Chloroplasts are artificial organelles created in laboratories — they do not exist naturally in any organism
Chloroplasts are identical to mitochondria in structure and function — both perform photosynthesis and cellular respiration interchangeably

quiz_questions.explanation: Chloroplasts and endosymbiotic theory: (1) Structure: double membrane (outer smooth, inner with invaginations), thylakoids (stacked into grana — site of light reactions, containing chlorophyll and photosystems I/II), stroma (fluid matrix — site of Calvin cycle/carbon fixation). Contain own circular DNA (~120-150 genes), 70S ribosomes, divide by binary fission. (2) Endosymbiotic theory (Lynn Margulis, 1967 paper 'On the Origin of Mitosing Cells'): ~1.5-2 billion years ago, ancestral heterotrophic eukaryote engulfed a photosynthetic cyanobacterium → endosymbiosis → over time, most genes transferred to host nucleus → cyanobacterium became chloroplast. Evidence: double membrane (inner = original cyanobacterial membrane, outer = host's phagocytic vacuole membrane), circular DNA (like bacteria), 70S ribosomes (bacterial-type, not 80S eukaryotic), binary fission (like bacteria), size similar to cyanobacteria (~1-10 μm), phylogenetics (chloroplast rRNA most closely related to cyanobacterial rRNA), sensitivity to antibiotics that target bacterial ribosomes. (3) Significance: photosynthesis by chloroplasts produces virtually all O₂ in Earth's atmosphere and is the foundation of most food chains.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are mitochondria, and what role do they play in cellular energy production?

options.text: Mitochondria are single-membrane organelles that perform photosynthesis to produce glucose — they contain chlorophyll

Mitochondria are double-membrane organelles (outer membrane + highly folded inner membrane forming cristae) that are the primary site of aerobic cellular respiration — they convert chemical energy from glucose and other organic molecules into ATP through the citric acid cycle (in the matrix) and oxidative phosphorylation (electron transport chain + chemiosmosis on the inner membrane) — like chloroplasts, they have their own circular DNA and 70S ribosomes, supporting the endosymbiotic theory that they evolved from engulfed aerobic bacteria (~1.5-2 billion years ago, proposed by Lynn Margulis)
Mitochondria are found only in plant cells, where they synthesize cellulose for the cell wall
Mitochondria are the same organelle as lysosomes — both digest cellular waste using hydrolytic enzymes at acidic pH

quiz_questions.explanation: Mitochondria — the powerhouse of the cell: (1) Structure: double membrane. Outer membrane (smooth, porin channels). Inner membrane (highly folded into cristae — increases surface area for ATP synthesis). Intermembrane space (between membranes — H⁺ ions accumulate here). Matrix (innermost compartment — contains mtDNA, 70S ribosomes, enzymes for citric acid cycle). (2) Function: aerobic respiration — glycolysis (cytoplasm) → pyruvate → matrix for citric acid cycle → NADH/FADH₂ → electron transport chain on inner membrane → chemiosmosis (ATP synthase uses H⁺ gradient to make ATP). ~30-32 ATP per glucose. (3) Endosymbiotic origin: originally free-living α-proteobacteria engulfed by ancestral eukaryote. Evidence: double membrane, circular DNA, 70S ribosomes, binary fission, phylogenetics. (4) Special features: maternal inheritance (mtDNA from mother only), high mutation rate, used in population genetics and forensics. (5) Mitochondrial diseases: mutations in mtDNA or nuclear genes for mitochondrial proteins → defective ATP production → especially affects high-energy-demand tissues (brain, heart, muscle). Examples: Leber's hereditary optic neuropathy, mitochondrial myopathy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the three components of the cytoskeleton, and how do they differ in structure and function?

options.text: The cytoskeleton is a rigid, permanent structure made entirely of bone-like material — it never changes shape and has a single component

The three cytoskeletal components are: (1) Microtubules (largest, ~25 nm diameter, hollow tubes of α/β-tubulin — functions: cell shape, intracellular transport via kinesin/dynein motor proteins, mitotic spindle formation, structure of cilia and flagella with 9+2 arrangement); (2) Microfilaments/actin filaments (smallest, ~7 nm, two twisted actin chains — functions: cell shape, muscle contraction with myosin, cell movement via pseudopods, cytokinesis via contractile ring); (3) Intermediate filaments (~8-12 nm, fibrous proteins like keratin/vimentin/lamin — functions: mechanical strength, anchoring nucleus and organelles, resist tensile stress)
The cytoskeleton consists only of cellulose microfibrils and is found exclusively in plant cells — animal cells lack internal structural support
The cytoskeleton is made of RNA molecules that fold into 3D scaffolds inside the cell — it has no protein components

quiz_questions.explanation: The cytoskeleton: a dynamic network of protein filaments. (1) Microtubules: largest (25 nm). Hollow tubes of α/β-tubulin heterodimers. Dynamic instability: constantly growing/shrinking from plus end. Functions: maintain cell shape, intracellular transport (kinesin = + end, dynein = − end), mitotic spindle (chromosome separation), cilia/flagella (9+2 axoneme arrangement — 9 doublet microtubules + 2 central singlets, dynein arms power bending). Colchicine/taxol: drugs that disrupt microtubules → used as anticancer agents. (2) Microfilaments (actin): smallest (7 nm). Two intertwined actin chains. Functions: cell shape (cortex beneath membrane), muscle contraction (actin-myosin interaction — sliding filament model), amoeboid movement (pseudopod extension), cytokinesis (cleavage furrow — contractile ring of actin/myosin). Cytochalasin: disrupts actin. (3) Intermediate filaments: medium (8-12 nm). Various proteins (keratins in epithelial cells, vimentin in connective tissue, neurofilaments in neurons, lamins in nuclear lamina). Most stable — rope-like tensile strength. Functions: mechanical support, nuclear anchoring, tissue-specific (e.g., epidermolysis bullosa from keratin mutations).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the function of the large central vacuole in plant cells, and how does it compare to vacuoles in other cell types?

options.text: The central vacuole produces ATP for the plant cell — it is the plant equivalent of mitochondria and is absent in all non-plant cells

The large central vacuole (tonoplast membrane) can occupy up to 80-90% of plant cell volume — it stores water, ions, nutrients, pigments (anthocyanins for flower color), and waste products — its water content generates turgor pressure against the cell wall, providing structural support and driving cell expansion during growth — other organisms also have vacuoles: food vacuoles (protists — phagocytosis/digestion), contractile vacuoles (freshwater protists — osmoregulation), and small storage vacuoles in animal cells
The central vacuole is a solid structure made of cellulose that provides rigidity — it has no liquid contents and plays no role in storage
Vacuoles are found only in bacterial cells where they serve as the nucleoid region for DNA storage

quiz_questions.explanation: Vacuoles: membrane-bound storage compartments. (1) Plant central vacuole: large (up to 80-90% of cell volume), bounded by tonoplast membrane. Contents: water (turgor pressure), ions (K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺ for osmotic regulation), sugars, amino acids, organic acids (citric acid in citrus fruits), pigments (anthocyanins — red/blue/purple flower colors), defensive compounds (alkaloids, tannins — deter herbivores), waste products. Functions: turgor pressure (structural support in non-woody plants, drives cell expansion during growth), storage (nutrients, pigments, defensive chemicals), recycling (vacuolar enzymes break down proteins/organelles, similar to lysosomes), pH regulation (acidic vacuole pH maintained by H⁺ ATPase). (2) Food vacuoles: protists and phagocytic cells — formed by endocytosis → fuse with lysosomes → digest contents. (3) Contractile vacuoles: freshwater protists (Paramecium, Amoeba) — pump out excess water entering by osmosis → osmoregulation. (4) Animal cell vacuoles: smaller and more numerous — temporary storage.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are ribosomes, where are they found, and what is their function in protein synthesis?

options.text: Ribosomes are membrane-bound organelles that produce lipids in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum — they have no role in protein synthesis

Ribosomes are non-membrane-bound molecular machines composed of ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and proteins that translate mRNA into polypeptide chains (protein synthesis) — eukaryotic ribosomes are 80S (60S + 40S subunits) while prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S (50S + 30S) — free ribosomes in the cytoplasm make proteins for use within the cell, while bound ribosomes on the rough ER make proteins destined for membranes, secretion, or lysosomes — rRNA is synthesized in the nucleolus, and ribosome subunits are assembled there before export through nuclear pores
Ribosomes are found only in the nucleus and function exclusively in DNA replication during cell division
Ribosomes are solid protein crystals that store amino acids until the cell needs them for energy production

quiz_questions.explanation: Ribosomes — protein synthesis machines: (1) Composition: ~60% rRNA + ~40% protein. NOT membrane-bound (not organelles in the strict sense). (2) Size: eukaryotic = 80S (60S large subunit + 40S small subunit). Prokaryotic/mitochondrial/chloroplast = 70S (50S + 30S). 'S' = Svedberg sedimentation coefficient (not additive). (3) Assembly: rRNA genes transcribed in nucleolus → rRNA combined with ribosomal proteins (imported from cytoplasm) → subunits assembled → exported through nuclear pores → join in cytoplasm during translation initiation. (4) Locations: free ribosomes (cytoplasm — make cytoplasmic, nuclear, peroxisomal, mitochondrial proteins), bound ribosomes (attached to rough ER via signal sequence/SRP pathway — make membrane, secretory, and lysosomal proteins). Same ribosome can be free or bound depending on the mRNA being translated. (5) Clinical: difference between 70S and 80S ribosomes exploited by antibiotics (tetracycline, erythromycin, chloramphenicol, streptomycin target 70S → kill bacteria without harming human 80S ribosomes — selective toxicity).

quiz_questions.question_text: What are the key structural components and functions of the nucleus, and how does the nuclear envelope regulate molecular traffic?

options.text: The nucleus is a solid structure without any pores or openings — molecules enter and exit by diffusing through the nuclear membrane like they cross the plasma membrane

The nucleus is bounded by a double-membrane nuclear envelope (continuous with the rough ER) perforated by ~3,000-4,000 nuclear pore complexes (NPCs, made of ~30 different nucleoporins) that regulate bidirectional molecular traffic — small molecules pass freely, but proteins >40 kDa require nuclear localization signals (NLS) for import and nuclear export signals (NES) for export, mediated by importins/exportins — the nucleus contains chromatin (DNA + histones), the nucleolus (rRNA synthesis and ribosome subunit assembly), and the nuclear lamina (intermediate filaments lining the inner membrane for structural support)
The nucleus contains only proteins and has no DNA — genetic information is stored in the mitochondria instead
The nuclear envelope is a single membrane identical to the plasma membrane — it has no special features for molecular transport

quiz_questions.explanation: The nucleus — information center: (1) Nuclear envelope: double membrane, continuous with rough ER at places. Outer membrane: has ribosomes (like RER). Inner membrane: associated with nuclear lamina. (2) Nuclear pore complexes (NPCs): ~3,000-4,000 per nucleus, each ~125 MDa, made of ~30 nucleoporins. Central channel ~5.2 nm for passive diffusion (ions, small molecules). Active transport of large molecules: proteins with NLS (nuclear localization signal, e.g., PKKKRKV) → recognized by importin → transported through NPC using Ran-GTPase energy system. mRNA export: processed mRNA bound by export factors → exportin → NPC → cytoplasm. (3) Chromatin: DNA wrapped around histone octamers → nucleosomes (beads on a string). Euchromatin (loose, active transcription) vs. heterochromatin (condensed, inactive). During cell division: chromatin condenses → visible chromosomes. (4) Nucleolus: site of rRNA transcription (RNA Pol I) and ribosome subunit assembly. Can be 1 or more per nucleus. Largest during active growth. (5) Nuclear lamina: meshwork of intermediate filaments (lamins A, B, C) lining inner membrane. Supports nuclear shape, anchors chromatin, organizes nuclear pores. Lamin mutations → laminopathies (e.g., Hutchinson-Gilford progeria — premature aging).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the endoplasmic reticulum?

options.text: A stack of flattened membrane sacs that modifies, sorts, and packages proteins for transport throughout the cell

An extensive network of membrane-enclosed tubules and sacs (cisternae) within the cytoplasm — the rough ER (studded with ribosomes) synthesizes, folds, and modifies proteins destined for membranes, secretion, or lysosomes, while the smooth ER (lacking ribosomes) synthesizes lipids, steroid hormones, detoxifies drugs and poisons, and stores calcium ions — the ER is continuous with the nuclear envelope and is the starting point of the secretory pathway
A double-membrane organelle that produces ATP through chemiosmosis and oxidative phosphorylation during cellular respiration
A membrane-bound vesicle containing hydrolytic enzymes that digest cellular waste at acidic pH

quiz_questions.explanation: Endoplasmic reticulum (ER): an extensive membrane system continuous with the nuclear envelope. Two types: Rough ER (RER) — ribosomes on cytoplasmic surface. Synthesizes membrane and secretory proteins. Signal sequence → SRP → RER translocon → protein enters lumen. Folding (chaperones: BiP, calnexin), N-linked glycosylation, quality control (misfolded proteins → ERAD = ER-associated degradation → proteasome). Smooth ER (SER) — no ribosomes. Lipid synthesis (phospholipids, cholesterol → distributed to all membranes via vesicles and lipid transfer proteins). Steroid hormone synthesis (adrenal cortex, gonads). Drug detoxification (cytochrome P450 enzymes in liver). Ca²⁺ storage (sarcoplasmic reticulum in muscle). ER stress: accumulation of misfolded proteins → unfolded protein response (UPR) → if unresolved → apoptosis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the Golgi apparatus?

options.text: A network of membrane tubules where proteins are synthesized by ribosomes and initially folded

A stack of flattened membrane cisternae organized with a cis (receiving) face and a trans (shipping) face — it receives proteins and lipids from the endoplasmic reticulum, further modifies them (glycosylation, phosphorylation), sorts them by adding molecular 'address tags' (e.g., mannose-6-phosphate for lysosomal enzymes), and packages them into vesicles for delivery to their final destinations — discovered by Camillo Golgi (Nobel Prize 1906)
A double-membrane organelle in plant cells where photosynthesis occurs, containing chlorophyll pigments
A structure within the nucleus that produces ribosomal RNA and assembles ribosome subunits

quiz_questions.explanation: Golgi apparatus: stack of 4-8 flattened membrane cisternae. Cis face (receiving, near ER) → medial → trans face (shipping, near plasma membrane). Functions: modification (O-linked glycosylation, trimming/modification of N-linked sugars from ER, phosphorylation — mannose-6-phosphate tag), sorting (address tags direct cargo), packaging (vesicles bud from trans-Golgi network to: plasma membrane, lysosomes, secretory granules). Vesicle coat proteins: COPII (ER → Golgi), COPI (Golgi → ER retrograde), clathrin (TGN → lysosomes/endosomes). Discovered by Camillo Golgi (1898) using his black reaction (silver nitrate staining of nerve tissue). Nobel Prize 1906.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a lysosome?

options.text: An organelle that produces energy by breaking down glucose through the electron transport chain and oxidative phosphorylation

A membrane-bound organelle containing approximately 50 different hydrolytic enzymes (proteases, lipases, nucleases, glycosidases) that operate at an acidic pH (~4.5-5.0, maintained by proton pumps) — lysosomes digest materials from endocytosis, phagocytosis, and autophagy — discovered by Christian de Duve (Nobel Prize 1974) — deficiency in lysosomal enzymes causes lysosomal storage diseases such as Tay-Sachs and Gaucher disease
A storage compartment found only in plant cells that contains water, pigments, and maintains turgor pressure
A ribosome-studded membrane network where proteins are translated from mRNA and initially folded into their 3D conformations

quiz_questions.explanation: Lysosome: the cell's digestive compartment. Membrane-bound, single membrane. ~50 acid hydrolases (optimal pH 4.5-5.0). pH maintained by V-type H⁺ ATPase (proton pump). Functions: heterophagy (digest extracellular material from endocytosis/phagocytosis), autophagy (recycle damaged organelles — Ohsumi, Nobel 2016), intracellular digestion. Formation: enzymes made on RER → Golgi adds M6P tag → sorted to lysosome. Safety: if lysosome ruptures, enzymes inactive at pH 7.2. Lysosomal storage diseases (~50): Tay-Sachs (hexosaminidase A ↓ → GM2 ganglioside accumulates in neurons → neurodegeneration), Gaucher (glucocerebrosidase ↓), Pompe (acid maltase ↓ → glycogen accumulates in muscle), Fabry (α-galactosidase A ↓). Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy (recombinant enzyme infusions).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a chloroplast?

options.text: An organelle found in all eukaryotic cells that breaks down glucose to produce ATP through aerobic respiration

A double-membrane organelle found in plant and algal cells that is the site of photosynthesis — it contains an internal thylakoid membrane system (organized into stacked grana) where the light reactions capture solar energy, and a fluid stroma where the Calvin cycle fixes CO₂ into organic molecules — chloroplasts contain chlorophyll pigments, their own circular DNA, and 70S ribosomes, supporting the endosymbiotic theory that they evolved from engulfed cyanobacteria (Lynn Margulis, 1967)
A membrane-enclosed sac containing digestive enzymes that operate at pH 4.5 to break down cellular waste
A fibrous protein network that maintains cell shape, powers cell movement, and separates chromosomes during cell division

quiz_questions.explanation: Chloroplast: site of photosynthesis in plants and algae. Structure: outer membrane (permeable to small molecules), inner membrane (selective transport), thylakoid membrane system (grana — stacks of disc-shaped thylakoids — connected by stroma lamellae), stroma (fluid matrix). Photosynthesis: light reactions (thylakoid membranes — photosystem II → water splitting/O₂ release → electron transport → photosystem I → NADPH + chemiosmosis → ATP), Calvin cycle (stroma — CO₂ fixation by RuBisCO → G3P → glucose). Pigments: chlorophyll a (P680, P700), chlorophyll b, carotenoids (accessory pigments, photoprotection). Endosymbiotic origin: from cyanobacteria (~1.5-2 BYA). Evidence: double membrane, circular DNA (~120-150 genes), 70S ribosomes, binary fission, phylogenetics. Significance: produces virtually all O₂ in Earth's atmosphere, foundation of most food chains, absorbs CO₂ (carbon sink).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a vacuole?

options.text: A protein synthesis complex composed of rRNA and ribosomal proteins that translates mRNA into polypeptide chains

A membrane-bound storage compartment — the large central vacuole in plant cells (bounded by the tonoplast membrane, occupying up to 80-90% of cell volume) stores water, ions, nutrients, pigments, and waste while generating turgor pressure essential for structural support — other types include food vacuoles (endocytic digestion in protists), contractile vacuoles (osmoregulation in freshwater protists), and smaller storage vacuoles in animal cells
A structure made of tubulin proteins that forms the mitotic spindle and separates chromosomes during cell division
The outer protective layer of a plant cell composed of cellulose microfibrils that provides rigidity and prevents excessive water uptake

quiz_questions.explanation: Vacuole: membrane-bound storage compartment. Plant central vacuole: bounded by tonoplast membrane. Occupies up to 80-90% of mature plant cell volume. Stores: water (turgor pressure), ions (K⁺, Cl⁻, Ca²⁺), sugars, amino acids, organic acids, pigments (anthocyanins), defensive compounds (alkaloids, tannins), waste products. Functions: turgor pressure (structural support → wilting = turgor loss; cell expansion during growth), storage, recycling (contains hydrolases similar to lysosomes), pH regulation. Other vacuoles: food vacuoles (protists/phagocytes — endocytic digestion), contractile vacuoles (freshwater protists — Paramecium — osmoregulation), gas vacuoles (some aquatic bacteria — buoyancy), smaller storage vacuoles in animal cells.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the endomembrane system?

options.text: A collection of organelles that all produce ATP, including mitochondria, chloroplasts, and peroxisomes

A network of interconnected membrane-bound organelles — including the nuclear envelope, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, vesicles, and the plasma membrane — that work together through vesicle trafficking to synthesize, modify, sort, and deliver proteins and lipids throughout the cell and to the cell surface
The collection of all DNA-containing organelles in the cell, specifically the nucleus, mitochondria, and chloroplasts
A protein fiber network consisting of microtubules, microfilaments, and intermediate filaments that maintains cell shape

quiz_questions.explanation: Endomembrane system: organelles connected by direct physical continuity or vesicle transfer. Components: nuclear envelope (continuous with RER), rough ER (protein synthesis), smooth ER (lipid synthesis, detox), Golgi apparatus (modification, sorting, packaging), lysosomes (digestion), vesicles (transport), plasma membrane (exocytosis/endocytosis). The secretory pathway: nucleus → RER → COPII vesicles → Golgi cis → trans-Golgi network → clathrin/secretory vesicles → destination. NOT included: mitochondria and chloroplasts (endosymbiotic origin, own DNA/ribosomes), peroxisomes (may derive from ER but functionally independent). Nobel recognition: Palade (1974 — secretory pathway), Rothman/Schekman/Südhof (2013 — vesicle trafficking mechanisms).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the cytoskeleton?

options.text: A rigid external shell made of calcium carbonate that protects the cell from physical damage, similar to the exoskeleton of arthropods

A dynamic network of protein filaments in the cytoplasm — consisting of microtubules (25 nm, tubulin — cell shape, transport, spindle, cilia/flagella), microfilaments (7 nm, actin — cell shape, movement, cytokinesis, muscle contraction), and intermediate filaments (8-12 nm, various proteins like keratin — mechanical strength, tensile resistance) — the cytoskeleton maintains cell shape, enables cell movement, drives intracellular transport, and powers cell division
A membrane-enclosed system of tubules where proteins are synthesized and lipids are manufactured in the cytoplasm
A layer of cellulose fibers found only in plant cells that provides rigid structural support outside the plasma membrane

quiz_questions.explanation: Cytoskeleton: dynamic protein network providing structure, movement, and organization. Three components: (1) Microtubules (25 nm) — α/β-tubulin polymers. Dynamic instability (grow/shrink rapidly). Functions: cell shape, intracellular transport (kinesin toward + end, dynein toward − end), mitotic spindle (chromosome separation), cilia/flagella (9+2 axoneme). Drug targets: colchicine, taxol (paclitaxel), vincristine → anticancer (disrupt mitotic spindle). (2) Microfilaments/actin (7 nm) — two twisted F-actin chains. Functions: cortical network (cell shape, membrane support), muscle contraction (actin-myosin sliding filament), amoeboid movement (pseudopods), cytokinesis (contractile ring). Drug: cytochalasin. (3) Intermediate filaments (8-12 nm) — various proteins (keratins, vimentin, neurofilaments, lamins). Most stable, rope-like. Functions: mechanical strength, anchoring organelles/nucleus. Mutations: epidermolysis bullosa simplex (keratin), laminopathies (lamin A/C).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a mitochondrion?

options.text: An organelle in plant cells where photosynthesis captures light energy and converts it into glucose and oxygen

A double-membrane organelle that is the primary site of aerobic cellular respiration — the inner membrane (folded into cristae to increase surface area) houses the electron transport chain and ATP synthase, while the matrix contains enzymes for the citric acid cycle — mitochondria have their own circular DNA and 70S ribosomes, supporting the endosymbiotic theory that they evolved from engulfed α-proteobacteria approximately 1.5-2 billion years ago
A single-membrane organelle containing hydrolytic enzymes that digest waste materials at an acidic pH of 4.5
A non-membrane-bound complex of rRNA and proteins that translates mRNA into polypeptide chains in the cytoplasm

quiz_questions.explanation: Mitochondrion: the 'powerhouse of the cell.' Double membrane: outer (porin channels, permeable to small molecules), inner (cristae — extensive folds, houses ETC complexes I-IV + ATP synthase, impermeable to most molecules → creates H⁺ gradient for chemiosmosis). Matrix: citric acid cycle enzymes, mtDNA, 70S ribosomes, beta-oxidation of fatty acids. ATP production: glycolysis (cytoplasm, 2 ATP) → pyruvate oxidation → citric acid cycle (matrix, 2 ATP + NADH/FADH₂) → ETC/oxidative phosphorylation (inner membrane, ~26-28 ATP) = ~30-32 ATP total per glucose. Endosymbiotic origin: from α-proteobacteria (~1.5-2 BYA). Maternal inheritance (mtDNA). Mitochondrial diseases affect high-energy tissues (brain, heart, muscle). Also involved in: apoptosis (cytochrome c release), calcium homeostasis, iron-sulfur cluster synthesis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the nucleolus?

options.text: A membrane-bound organelle in the cytoplasm that sorts and packages proteins into vesicles for transport

A dense, non-membrane-bound structure within the nucleus where ribosomal RNA (rRNA) is synthesized by RNA polymerase I and where ribosome subunits are partially assembled before being exported through nuclear pores to the cytoplasm — the nucleolus forms around nucleolar organizer regions (NORs) on specific chromosomes and is prominent in cells with high rates of protein synthesis
The region of the cytoplasm between the plasma membrane and the nuclear envelope, containing all organelles and the cytosol
A whip-like projection from the cell surface made of microtubules in a 9+2 arrangement used for cell locomotion

quiz_questions.explanation: Nucleolus: dense region within the nucleus (not membrane-bound). Function: rRNA synthesis (RNA Pol I transcribes 45S pre-rRNA → processed into 28S, 18S, 5.8S rRNA) and ribosome subunit assembly. rRNA genes (rDNA): located at nucleolar organizer regions (NORs) on chromosomes 13, 14, 15, 21, 22 in humans (~400 copies — multiple copies to produce massive rRNA amounts). Assembly: rRNA + ribosomal proteins (made in cytoplasm, imported) → partial assembly of 60S and 40S subunits → exported through nuclear pores → final assembly in cytoplasm during translation initiation. Size: prominent in cells with high protein synthesis rates (secretory cells, cancer cells — nucleolar size correlates with growth rate). Can have 1 or more nucleoli per nucleus. Disappears during mitosis (chromatin condensation) and reforms after cell division.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the extracellular matrix (ECM)?

options.text: The fluid inside the cell between the plasma membrane and the nuclear envelope — it contains all organelles and dissolved molecules

A complex network of proteins and carbohydrates secreted by animal cells that lies outside the plasma membrane — major components include collagen (structural strength), proteoglycans (hydration and compression resistance), fibronectin and laminin (connect ECM to cell surface integrins) — the ECM provides structural support, regulates cell behavior (growth, differentiation, migration), facilitates cell-to-cell signaling, and is essential for tissue integrity
The rigid cell wall made of cellulose found exclusively in plant cells that prevents over-expansion in hypotonic solutions
A mesh of intermediate filament proteins lining the inner surface of the nuclear envelope that provides structural support to the nucleus

quiz_questions.explanation: Extracellular matrix (ECM): the structural and signaling network outside animal cells. Components: (1) Collagen: most abundant protein in the body (~30% of total protein). Triple helix of three α chains. Types: Type I (bone, skin, tendon — tensile strength), Type II (cartilage), Type IV (basement membranes). (2) Proteoglycans: core protein + glycosaminoglycans (GAGs: hyaluronic acid, chondroitin sulfate, heparan sulfate). Highly hydrated → resist compressive forces (cartilage). (3) Fibronectin: connects ECM to cells via integrins (transmembrane receptors). (4) Laminin: major component of basement membranes. Functions: structural support, cell adhesion via integrins (outside-in and inside-out signaling), regulation of cell growth/differentiation/migration/survival, wound healing, tissue development. ECM remodeling: matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) degrade ECM — important in development, wound healing, and (pathologically) in cancer metastasis. Plant equivalent: cell wall (cellulose microfibrils, hemicellulose, pectin, lignin).

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient is diagnosed with Tay-Sachs disease, a fatal genetic disorder in which the lysosomal enzyme hexosaminidase A is deficient. The patient's neurons accumulate massive amounts of GM2 ganglioside, leading to progressive neurological deterioration. Explain how a defective lysosomal enzyme causes this disease, and why neurons are particularly affected.

options.text: Hexosaminidase A is a mitochondrial enzyme — its deficiency prevents ATP production specifically in neurons, causing energy failure and cell death

Normally, lysosomes use hexosaminidase A to break down GM2 ganglioside (a glycolipid component of neuronal membranes) — without this enzyme, GM2 accumulates in lysosomes → lysosomes swell enormously (up to 10× normal size) → disrupts normal cellular function → triggers apoptosis — neurons are particularly vulnerable because they have the highest turnover of gangliosides (enriched in brain tissue) and are post-mitotic (cannot divide to dilute accumulating substrate) — this is a lysosomal storage disease demonstrating that lysosomes are essential for ongoing cellular recycling
Tay-Sachs is caused by a defective ribosome that cannot synthesize gangliosides — the accumulation occurs because the cell overproduces lipids in the smooth ER
GM2 ganglioside accumulates in the Golgi apparatus because without hexosaminidase A, the Golgi cannot package proteins correctly — lysosomes are unaffected

quiz_questions.explanation: Tay-Sachs disease — lysosomal storage disease: (1) Cause: autosomal recessive mutations in HEXA gene → deficient hexosaminidase A enzyme → cannot cleave GM2 ganglioside in lysosomes. (2) Pathology: GM2 ganglioside is a normal glycolipid component of neuronal membranes. Neurons continuously internalize and recycle membrane lipids → gangliosides delivered to lysosomes for degradation. Without hexosaminidase A → GM2 accumulates → lysosomes swell (membranous cytoplasmic bodies) → disrupts axonal transport, synaptic function, and triggers inflammation/apoptosis. (3) Why neurons? Highest ganglioside content of any cell type. Post-mitotic — cannot divide to dilute substrate. High metabolic rate. Long axons vulnerable to transport disruption. (4) Clinical: infantile form (most severe) — normal at birth → developmental regression at 3-6 months → seizures, blindness (cherry-red spot on retina), death by age 3-5. (5) Prevalence: Ashkenazi Jewish population (~1/30 carrier frequency). (6) This demonstrates that lysosomal function is not just 'garbage disposal' — it's essential for continuous cellular homeostasis. ~50 known lysosomal storage diseases collectively affect ~1/7,700 births.

quiz_questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company is developing a cancer drug that targets microtubules. They know that taxol (paclitaxel) from Pacific yew trees and vincristine from periwinkle plants both disrupt microtubule dynamics but in opposite ways. How does each drug affect microtubules, and why does disrupting microtubules kill cancer cells?

options.text: Both drugs dissolve the plasma membrane of cancer cells — microtubules are irrelevant to cancer cell death

Taxol stabilizes microtubules (prevents depolymerization → microtubules cannot shorten), while vincristine destabilizes microtubules (prevents polymerization → microtubules cannot grow) — both approaches prevent the dynamic assembly/disassembly of the mitotic spindle that is essential for chromosome separation during cell division → cells are arrested in mitosis → cannot complete cell division → apoptosis — rapidly dividing cancer cells are particularly vulnerable because they undergo frequent mitosis
Both drugs enhance microtubule assembly, making the mitotic spindle extra strong and causing cells to divide too rapidly → cell exhaustion → death
These drugs target intermediate filaments, not microtubules — they destroy the structural framework of the cell, causing it to collapse

quiz_questions.explanation: Microtubule-targeting anticancer drugs: (1) Normal mitosis: microtubules of the mitotic spindle undergo rapid assembly (polymerization) and disassembly (depolymerization) — this dynamic instability is essential for: capturing chromosomes at kinetochores, aligning chromosomes at metaphase plate, separating sister chromatids during anaphase. (2) Taxol (paclitaxel, from Taxus brevifolia): binds β-tubulin → stabilizes microtubules → prevents depolymerization → 'frozen' spindle cannot function dynamically → mitotic arrest at metaphase → apoptosis. Used for: breast, ovarian, lung cancers. (3) Vincristine/vinblastine (from Catharanthus roseus): bind tubulin dimers → prevent polymerization → microtubules cannot assemble → no functional spindle → mitotic arrest → apoptosis. Used for: lymphomas, leukemias. (4) Both exploit the same vulnerability: dividing cells require dynamic microtubules. Cancer cells divide more frequently → more opportunities to encounter the drug during mitosis. (5) Side effects: affect all dividing cells (hair loss, bone marrow suppression, GI damage — because these tissues have rapidly dividing cells that also need microtubules for mitosis). Neuropathy (neurons depend on microtubule-based axonal transport).

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher uses cell fractionation and differential centrifugation to isolate organelles from liver cells. She subjects a cell homogenate to successive centrifugation spins at increasing speeds: 1,000g, 10,000g, 100,000g, and then ultracentrifugation of the final supernatant. What organelles are collected at each step, and what principle determines the separation?

options.text: All organelles pellet at the same speed regardless of their size — centrifugation cannot separate different organelles because they all have the same density

Organelles separate based on size and density — larger/denser organelles sediment at lower speeds: 1,000g pellets nuclei and cell debris (largest); 10,000g pellets mitochondria, chloroplasts, lysosomes, and peroxisomes; 100,000g pellets microsomes (ER and Golgi membrane fragments) and plasma membrane vesicles; ultracentrifugation of supernatant yields ribosomes and large macromolecular complexes — this technique was pioneered by Albert Claude and Christian de Duve (Nobel Prize 1974) and was essential for discovering the function of each organelle
Centrifugation separates organelles by their color — green organelles (chloroplasts) pellet first, followed by brown (mitochondria), then clear (ER)
Only the first spin at 1,000g is useful — all subsequent spins produce identical pellets containing mixed organelles

quiz_questions.explanation: Cell fractionation and differential centrifugation: (1) Principle: centrifugal force causes particles to sediment based on their sedimentation coefficient, which depends on size, shape, and density. Larger/denser organelles sediment at lower centrifugal forces. (2) Procedure: homogenize cells → sequential centrifugation at increasing speeds. (a) 1,000g (10 min): nuclei, unbroken cells, cell debris (P1 pellet). (b) 10,000g (20 min): mitochondria, chloroplasts (plant cells), lysosomes, peroxisomes (P2 pellet). (c) 100,000g (60 min): microsomes (rough ER and smooth ER membrane fragments), Golgi vesicles, plasma membrane fragments (P3 pellet). (d) Ultracentrifugation (>100,000g, 2-3 hrs): ribosomes, large macromolecular complexes (P4 pellet). Supernatant S4: cytosol (soluble proteins, small molecules). (3) Further purification: density gradient centrifugation (sucrose or CsCl gradients) for higher resolution separation. (4) Historical significance: Albert Claude (developed cell fractionation, 1930s-40s), Christian de Duve (discovered lysosomes and peroxisomes using this technique), George Palade (used EM + fractionation to elucidate secretory pathway) — all shared Nobel Prize in Physiology/Medicine, 1974.

quiz_questions.question_text: A child inherits a mutation in a gene for a nuclear pore complex protein (nucleoporin). As a result, nuclear transport of proteins and mRNA export are severely impaired. Predict the consequences for cellular function.

options.text: Nuclear pore mutations would have no effect because molecules can freely diffuse through the nuclear envelope's lipid bilayer without any pore structures
